description:
A storage administrator left you a JSON inventory of the current block devices and asked for a
  very quick consistency check before the next capacity-planning meeting.

  1. Location of the data  
     • The JSON file is already on the system:  
       /home/user/storage/disks.json  

  2. Validation requirement  
     • Every element of the top-level array must be an object that contains BOTH of these
       properties:  
         name    – type: string  
         size_gb – type: number  
     • The administrator wants a boolean-style result, not a list of failures.  
       When all objects are compliant, write the single line  
         JSON_VALID  
       otherwise write  
         JSON_INVALID  

  3. Where the result must go  
     • Write the result of the check to the file  
       /home/user/reports/disk_validation.log  
     • The file must contain exactly one line terminated by a single UNIX newline
       (line-feed, “\n”).  
       Examples of valid file contents:  
         JSON_VALID\n  
         JSON_INVALID\n  
       No additional whitespace or lines are allowed.

  4. Constraints  
     • Accomplish the validation with ONE jq command executed from the shell.  
       (Anything that can be typed in one line is acceptable; no need for a wrapper script.)  
     • The command may use jq’s built-in type-testing facilities; no external libraries or
       schema validators are necessary.  
     • Redirect the command’s standard output to overwrite the log file specified above.  

  5. Hints (not mandatory to follow)  
     • Remember that jq has the “type” builtin and truthy / falsy logic that can be folded with
       “all”, “any”, “map”, etc.  
     • Exit status is irrelevant; only the exact contents of the log file matter for grading.

  The automated grader will:
     1. Read /home/user/reports/disk_validation.log  
     2. Verify that it contains exactly “JSON_VALID” (with a trailing newline) or
        “JSON_INVALID” (with a trailing newline) depending on whether the data comply.  

  No other changes to the filesystem are required.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (already present when the agent starts):

  1. Directory layout  
     /home/user/  
       ├── storage/  
       │     └── disks.json  
       └── reports/           (empty)

  2. File: /home/user/storage/disks.json  
     Contents (3 disk objects, all valid):
     [
       { "name": "sda", "size_gb": 950 },
       { "name": "sdb", "size_gb": 1200 },
       { "name": "sdc", "size_gb": 500 }
     ]

  3. Permissions  
     • All directories: 755 (user writable)  
     • Existing files:   644  
     • The agent runs as the regular user and can create or overwrite files in /home/user/reports.

  4. Expected grading behaviour  
     • Because every object in disks.json already meets the stated requirements,
       the correct output line is “JSON_VALID”.  
     • Therefore, after the agent has completed the task, the file
       /home/user/reports/disk_validation.log must exist and contain exactly:
       JSON_VALID\n          (five bytes: J S O N _ V A L I D LF)

  The grader will fail the task if:
     • The file is missing.  
     • The content differs (extra spaces, extra lines, wrong word, missing newline, etc.).  
     • Any other files are modified (not expected but checked).
